The **M5 Motorway** is a 28.8-kilometre (17.9 mi)[[1]](#cite_note-gmaps-1) series of [tolled](/wiki/Road_pricing "Road pricing") [motorways](/wiki/Motorway "Motorway") located in [Sydney](/wiki/Sydney "Sydney"), [New South Wales](/wiki/New_South_Wales "New South Wales"), designated as route M5. It is part of the [Sydney Orbital Network](/wiki/Sydney_Orbital_Network "Sydney Orbital Network").

The M5 Motorway comprises two connected parts:

* **South West Motorway** is a [toll road](/wiki/Road_pricing "Road pricing") operated by Interlink Roads, between the [Roden Cutler Interchange](/wiki/Sir_Roden_Cutler_VC_Memorial_Interchange "Sir Roden Cutler VC Memorial Interchange") in [Prestons](/wiki/Prestons,_New_South_Wales "Prestons, New South Wales") (where the M5 meets [Westlink M7](/wiki/Westlink_M7 "Westlink M7") and [Hume Motorway](/wiki/Hume_Highway "Hume Highway")) and the interchange with [King Georges Road](/wiki/King_Georges_Road "King Georges Road") at [Beverly Hills](/wiki/Beverly_Hills,_New_South_Wales "Beverly Hills, New South Wales"). The section between Hammondville and Beverly Hills is tolled under the M5 South West tolls.
* The **M5 East** is an above-ground road from King Georges Road east to Bexley Road which continues into a 4-kilometre (2.5 mi) tunnel from [Bexley North](/wiki/Bexley_North "Bexley North") to [Arncliffe](/wiki/Arncliffe,_New_South_Wales "Arncliffe, New South Wales") that opened in December 2001 to connect with [General Holmes Drive](/wiki/General_Holmes_Drive "General Holmes Drive") at [Mascot](/wiki/Mascot,_New_South_Wales "Mascot, New South Wales"). The section between King Georges Road and Marsh Street is tolled since July 2020.

The [M8 Motorway](/wiki/M8_Motorway_(Sydney) "M8 Motorway (Sydney)") also runs roughly parallel to the M5 East and links it at Kingsgrove to the [Rozelle Interchange](/wiki/Rozelle_Interchange "Rozelle Interchange") along separate twin tunnels. Both the M5 East and M8 motorway are tolled as part of [WestConnex](/wiki/WestConnex "WestConnex"), separate to the M5 South West tolls. This results in two separate tolls when travelling along the M5 between Moorebank and Mascot.

## Route

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=M5_Motorway_(Sydney)&action=edit&section=1 "Edit section: Route")]

South West Motorway commences at the [Roden Cutler Interchange](/wiki/Sir_Roden_Cutler_VC_Memorial_Interchange "Sir Roden Cutler VC Memorial Interchange") (where it meets [Hume Motorway](/wiki/Hume_Highway "Hume Highway") and [Westlink M7](/wiki/Westlink_M7 "Westlink M7")) in [Prestons](/wiki/Prestons,_New_South_Wales "Prestons, New South Wales") and heads in a northeasterly direction as a six-lane, dual-carriageway road, widening to 8 lanes at the interchange with Hume Highway in southern [Liverpool](/wiki/Liverpool,_New_South_Wales "Liverpool, New South Wales") and heading in an easterly direction, crossing the [Main Southern railway line](/wiki/Main_Southern_railway_line "Main Southern railway line") and then the [Georges River](/wiki/Georges_River "Georges River") into [Moorebank](/wiki/Moorebank,_New_South_Wales "Moorebank, New South Wales"), then narrowing back to six lanes at the interchange with [Heathcote Road](/wiki/Heathcote_Road "Heathcote Road") at [Wattle Grove](/wiki/Wattle_Grove,_New_South_Wales "Wattle Grove, New South Wales"). It becomes the M5 East at the interchange with [King Georges Road](/wiki/King_Georges_Road "King Georges Road") in [Beverly Hills](/wiki/Beverly_Hills,_New_South_Wales "Beverly Hills, New South Wales"), and just east of this interchange it widens to ten lanes to accommodate the portals to the [M8 Motorway](/wiki/M8_Motorway_(Sydney) "M8 Motorway (Sydney)"), then narrows back to four lanes afterwards and enters the M5 East tunnels at the interchange with Bexley Road, emerging four kilometres later in [Arncliffe](/wiki/Arncliffe,_New_South_Wales "Arncliffe, New South Wales"). It then crosses under the [Cooks River](/wiki/Cooks_River "Cooks River") to eventually terminate with [General Holmes Drive](/wiki/General_Holmes_Drive "General Holmes Drive") at [Mascot](/wiki/Mascot,_New_South_Wales "Mascot, New South Wales").

The [M5 cycleway](/wiki/M5_cycleway "M5 cycleway") is located on the breakdown lanes of the M5 South West Motorway, and runs off-road parallel to the M5 East between King Georges Road and [Arncliffe](/wiki/Arncliffe,_New_South_Wales "Arncliffe, New South Wales").

## History

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=M5_Motorway_(Sydney)&action=edit&section=2 "Edit section: History")]

### South West Motorway

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=M5_Motorway_(Sydney)&action=edit&section=3 "Edit section: South West Motorway")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/9/9b/M5_South-West_Motorway_Logo.png/250px-M5_South-West_Motorway_Logo.png)](/wiki/File:M5_South-West_Motorway_Logo.png)

Logo of South West Motorway

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/c/cb/M5_Motorway_Sydney1.jpg/250px-M5_Motorway_Sydney1.jpg)](/wiki/File:M5_Motorway_Sydney1.jpg)

Looking eastbound towards the M8 tunnel portal

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/2/23/M5_Motorway_Sydney.jpg/250px-M5_Motorway_Sydney.jpg)](/wiki/File:M5_Motorway_Sydney.jpg)

Looking westbound near Kingsgrove

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/7/7a/M5_Motorway_Sydney_Revesby.jpg/250px-M5_Motorway_Sydney_Revesby.jpg)](/wiki/File:M5_Motorway_Sydney_Revesby.jpg)

Looking westbound from an overpass at [Revesby](/wiki/Revesby,_New_South_Wales "Revesby, New South Wales")

South West Motorway replaced Hume Highway (Liverpool Road) as the primary route from [Liverpool](/wiki/Liverpool,_New_South_Wales "Liverpool, New South Wales") to the [Sydney CBD](/wiki/Sydney_central_business_district "Sydney central business district"). In the 1980s, the [Hume Highway](/wiki/Hume_Highway "Hume Highway") (National Highway 31) ended at Crossroads, just before Liverpool. This stretch of freeway ([Campbelltown](/wiki/Campbelltown,_New_South_Wales "Campbelltown, New South Wales") to Crossroads) was previously known as South Western Freeway, designated F5.

The South Western Freeway is different to the M5 South West Motorway. From then on, to proceed to the city, the alternatives were either [Liverpool Road](/wiki/Hume_Highway "Hume Highway") (then National Route 31) or [Newbridge and Canterbury Roads](/wiki/A34_(Sydney) "A34 (Sydney)") (then State Route 54) via [Bankstown](/wiki/Bankstown "Bankstown"). Both routes had many traffic lights and were (and still are) heavily congested.

An initial stretch of the motorway was built and completed in February 1985 to link [Heathcote Road](/wiki/Heathcote_Road "Heathcote Road") at [Moorebank](/wiki/Moorebank,_New_South_Wales "Moorebank, New South Wales") and Hume Highway at [Casula](/wiki/Casula,_New_South_Wales "Casula, New South Wales"), including a bridge across the [Georges River](/wiki/Georges_River "Georges River") near Casula. It was built as a single carriageway and included a [traffic light](/wiki/Traffic_light "Traffic light") controlled, at-grade [intersection](/wiki/Intersection_(road) "Intersection (road)") at Moorebank Avenue.[[3]](#cite_note-m5sw-3)

After years of proposals and political promises,[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*] a privately constructed and operated motorway – the M5 South West Motorway – was built under a [Build-Operate-Transfer](/wiki/Build-Operate-Transfer "Build-Operate-Transfer") agreement, which was awarded to Interlink Roads.[[4]](#cite_note-2012treasury-4) The motorway was constructed on a publicly owned freeway reservation, between the existing road at Heathcote Road, Moorebank, to [King Georges Road](/wiki/King_Georges_Road "King Georges Road"), [Beverly Hills](/wiki/Beverly_Hills,_New_South_Wales "Beverly Hills, New South Wales"). It was constructed in two stages:[[4]](#cite_note-2012treasury-4)

* Stage 1: between Hume Highway and The River Road
* Stage 2: between The River Road and [King Georges Road](/wiki/King_Georges_Road "King Georges Road")

Stage 1 and a portion of Stage 2 (The River Road to [Fairford Road](/wiki/A6_(Sydney) "A6 (Sydney)")) of the motorway opened in August 1992, with the remaining portion of Stage 2 opened in October 1992.[[4]](#cite_note-2012treasury-4) The motorway was "completed" months ahead of schedule, even though the proposed link between the Cross Roads and Casula (Casula bypass) had yet to be constructed, and a third of the "motorway" existed only as single-carriageway road between the Fairford Road and King Georges Road interchanges, remarkably located at the busier city end of the road.[[3]](#cite_note-m5sw-3)

Under the original contract in 1991, Interlink Roads would operate and toll the motorway for a concession of 22 years after opening. This meant that the toll concession was originally scheduled to end in August 2014.[[4]](#cite_note-2012treasury-4)

Metroad 5 was introduced in late 1992 along with other Metroads and was signed in February 1993.[[5]](#cite_note-Met5-5) South West Motorway, from Beverly Hills to Moorebank and Crossroads to Campbelltown, along with Hume Highway, from Moorebank to Crossroads, were allocated Metroad 5. The Casula Bypass opened in June 1994, and the Hume Highway section of Metroad 5 was realigned to the bypass and the entire South West Motorway therefore had the route allocation of Metroad 5. As part of the Casula Bypass works, the toll concession was extended from August 2014 to June 2022.[[4]](#cite_note-2012treasury-4)

The passing of the *Main Roads Act of 1924*[[6]](#cite_note-6) through the [Parliament of New South Wales](/wiki/Parliament_of_New_South_Wales "Parliament of New South Wales") provided for the declaration of Main Roads, roads partially funded by the State government through the [Main Roads Board](/wiki/Main_Roads_Board "Main Roads Board"). With the subsequent passing of the *Main Roads (Amendment) Act of 1929*[[7]](#cite_note-7) to provide for additional declarations of State Highways and Trunk Roads, the [Roads & Traffic Authority](/wiki/Roads_%26_Traffic_Authority "Roads & Traffic Authority") (the eventual successor to the Main Roads Board) declared South West Motorway as a motorway (under plan number 6005), on 28 June 1993,[[2]](#cite_note-nswgovgaz93-2) and was re-declared to cover each extension until it reached its eastern terminus in Mascot in 2001; the tollway today still retains this declaration (under Tollway 6005).[[8]](#cite_note-nswroadsched-8)

As part of the construction of the M5 East, the eastern two-lane, single carriageway section of South West Motorway was widened to a full 4 lane, dual-carriageway road between June 1997 and August 1999, finally bringing most of the motorway up to complete freeway standard. This also included the opening of east-facing ramps and toll plazas at The River Road and Fairford Road.[[4]](#cite_note-2012treasury-4) However, the at-grade intersection at Moorebank Avenue still remained, posing a constraint on traffic flow. In January 2003, a grade-separated interchange at Moorebank Avenue was completed, meaning that not only the entire length of Metroad 5 was now freeway standard but also the entire route from [Canberra](/wiki/Canberra "Canberra") to Sydney, and [Melbourne](/wiki/Melbourne "Melbourne") to Sydney, was now high-speed dual carriageway.[[3]](#cite_note-m5sw-3) However, the toll concession was also extended from June 2022 to August 2023.[[4]](#cite_note-2012treasury-4)

With the completion of the [Westlink M7](/wiki/Westlink_M7 "Westlink M7") motorway in December 2005, the [Roden Cutler Interchange](/wiki/Sir_Roden_Cutler_VC_Memorial_Interchange "Sir Roden Cutler VC Memorial Interchange") was added at [Prestons](/wiki/Prestons,_New_South_Wales "Prestons, New South Wales") to allow traffic from the south to access western Sydney or bypass the city altogether.

Between 2012 and 2014, the motorway was widened to three lanes in each direction.[[9]](#cite_note-9) As part of widening works, the toll concession was extended for the third time to 10 December 2026.[[4]](#cite_note-2012treasury-4)

New eastward-facing ramps at Belmore Road were constructed and opened on 5 February 2019.[[10]](#cite_note-10)

#### Toll

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=M5_Motorway_(Sydney)&action=edit&section=4 "Edit section: Toll")]

The ownership of M5 South West Motorway is split between the state government and Interlink Roads, the latter is 100% owned by [Transurban](/wiki/Transurban "Transurban").[[11]](#cite_note-transurban2020-11)[[12]](#cite_note-12) The state government owns the section between Hume Highway and Heathcote Road, with Interlink Roads owning the remainder of the motorway including the Casula Bypass.[[3]](#cite_note-m5sw-3) However, the Casula Bypass is not tolled.

Interlink Roads was initially equally owned by [Leighton](/wiki/CIMIC_Group "CIMIC Group") and [Commonwealth Bank](/wiki/Commonwealth_Bank "Commonwealth Bank"). Leighton sold its 50% shareholding to [Macquarie Infrastructure Group](/wiki/Atlas_Arteria "Atlas Arteria") (MIG) in December 1996, while Commonwealth Bank sold 34.6% of its shareholding to [Utilities Trust of Australia](/wiki/Morrison_%26_Co "Morrison & Co") and its remaining 15.4% shareholding to [AMP Limited](/wiki/AMP_Limited "AMP Limited") in December 1998.[[4]](#cite_note-2012treasury-4) AMP in turn sold some of its shareholding to [Retail Employees Superannuation](/wiki/Rest_Super "Rest Super") and [Sunsuper](/wiki/Sunsuper "Sunsuper") in 1999.

Sydney Roads Group was spun off from Macquarie Infrastructure Group in June 2006, including its 50% stake in Interlink Roads.[[13]](#cite_note-13) [Transurban](/wiki/Transurban "Transurban") first acquired ownership of Interlink Roads in April 2007 when it acquired Sydney Roads Group. By 2012, the shareholders of Interlink Roads were:[[4]](#cite_note-2012treasury-4)

* Transurban (50%)
* [Utilities Trust of Australia](/wiki/Utilities_Trust_of_Australia "Utilities Trust of Australia") (UTA) (19.23%)
* [IFM Investors](/wiki/IFM_Investors "IFM Investors") (15.38%)
* [BNP Paribas](/wiki/BNP_Paribas "BNP Paribas") Nominees Pty Ltd (8.24%), formerly known as Cogent Nominees Pty Ltd (2002–2012) and AMP Nominees Pty Ltd (prior to 2002)
* [State Street Corporation](/wiki/State_Street_Corporation "State Street Corporation"), as trustees of [Retail Employees Superannuation](/wiki/Rest_Super "Rest Super") (4.4%) and [Sunsuper](/wiki/Sunsuper "Sunsuper") Superannuation Fund (2.75%)

Transurban gained majority control of Interlink Roads when it acquired BNP's 8.24% stake on 18 September 2018, before acquiring the 7.14% stakes from Rest Super and Sunsuper on 3 December 2018, increasing its shareholding to 65.38%.[[14]](#cite_note-14)[[15]](#cite_note-15) Transurban acquired the remaining 34.62% stake from IFM and Utilities Trust of Australia on 30 October 2019.[[11]](#cite_note-transurban2020-11)

The motorway east of Heathcote Road is tolled by Interlink Roads at a single price, with toll gantries located at [Hammondville](/wiki/Hammondville,_New_South_Wales "Hammondville, New South Wales") and at east-facing ramps at Henry Lawson Drive, The River Road, Fairford Road and Belmore Road. The motorway is tolled separately to the M5 East. Regular users of the motorway can claim a rebate from the state government for the cost of the toll, excluding GST, as part of a [1995 election](/wiki/1995_New_South_Wales_state_election "1995 New South Wales state election") commitment by [Bob Carr](/wiki/Bob_Carr "Bob Carr"), who eventually won the election and became [Premier of New South Wales](/wiki/Premier_of_New_South_Wales "Premier of New South Wales"). On 1 July 2013, the M5 South West became the last motorway in Australia to convert to cashless tolling.[[16]](#cite_note-16)

The current toll [concession](/wiki/Concession_(contract) "Concession (contract)") held by Interlink Roads is due to expire on 10 December 2026, after it was extended for three times since opening.[[17]](#cite_note-17)[[18]](#cite_note-fy19_asx-18) As part of the acquisition of [WestConnex](/wiki/WestConnex "WestConnex") by a [Transurban](/wiki/Transurban "Transurban")-led consortium, the M5 South West Motorway will be transferred to WestConnex ownership when the Interlink Roads concession ends in 2026,[[19]](#cite_note-19)[[18]](#cite_note-fy19_asx-18) and will then be tolled as part of WestConnex together with M5 East.[[20]](#cite_note-20)

The M5 East is the eastern section of the M5 between [King Georges Road](/wiki/King_Georges_Road "King Georges Road") at [Beverly Hills](/wiki/Beverly_Hills,_New_South_Wales "Beverly Hills, New South Wales") and [General Holmes Drive](/wiki/General_Holmes_Drive "General Holmes Drive") at [Mascot](/wiki/Mascot,_New_South_Wales "Mascot, New South Wales"), and includes two 4 km tunnels between [Bexley North](/wiki/Bexley_North,_New_South_Wales "Bexley North, New South Wales") and [Arncliffe](/wiki/Arncliffe,_New_South_Wales "Arncliffe, New South Wales"), and another tunnel under the [Cooks River](/wiki/Cooks_River "Cooks River").

After the Casula Bypass from Cross Roads to Casula was completed in 1994, the M5 South West Motorway still abruptly ended at King Georges Road, meaning that motorway traffic was forced onto congested roads to complete the journey into the city. [Roads & Traffic Authority](/wiki/Roads_%26_Traffic_Authority "Roads & Traffic Authority") (RTA) released the Environmental Impact Assessment of the M5 East Motorway for public consultation in June 1994, which proposed:[[26]](#cite_note-28)

* Duplicating the existing single-lane M5 South West Motorway (opened 1992) between Fairford Road and King Georges Road
* New tolled motorway between King Georges Road and General Holmes Drive at Mascot, adjacent to [Sydney Airport](/wiki/Sydney_Airport "Sydney Airport"), including tunnels between Bexley North and Turrella and a viaduct between Turrella and Marsh Street
* An overpass over King Georges Road, with the embankment to be filled by spoil from the tunnel excavation

The toll plaza was proposed to be located near Garema Circuit, Armitree Street and Glamis Street in Kingsgrove.

Many of the public submissions raised during the EIS consultation were concerned with the visual impact of the proposed elevated motorway through Turrella, and the impact on residences in Arncliffe. In response to comments, it was announced in August 1995 that RTA would examine alternatives to the proposal between Bexley Road and Cooks River. In December 1996, an alternative direct tunnel proposal was announced in a supplement report to the EIS (EIS supplement), which was exhibited for public consultation until February 1997. The alternative proposal proposed shorter and more direct tunnels between Bexley Road and Marsh Street.[[27]](#cite_note-29) There would also be no tolls imposed on the motorway, as announced by the Minister for Roads a month prior. The proposed toll plaza would then be used as heavy vehicle inspection areas instead.

In response to submissions during the EIS supplement consultation, the proposal was further modified, reverting to an underpass below King Georges Road and a tunnel underneath Cooks River.[[3]](#cite_note-m5sw-3) The modified proposal was approved in November 1997 and would be built as a publicly-built and toll-free link known as the M5 East Freeway.[[28]](#cite_note-oldm5east-30) Construction commenced in August 1998 and the M5 East was opened on 9 December 2001. Metroad 5 from [Beverly Hills](/wiki/Beverly_Hills,_New_South_Wales "Beverly Hills, New South Wales") to the CBD, originally via [Hume](/wiki/Hume_Highway "Hume Highway") and [Great Western Highway](/wiki/Great_Western_Highway "Great Western Highway"), was rerouted on the M5 East Freeway and ended at its terminus at [General Holmes Drive](/wiki/General_Holmes_Drive "General Holmes Drive").[[5]](#cite_note-Met5-5)

Controversy surrounded the construction of the M5 East concerning the effect of the freeway on the local environment, especially upon parts of [Wolli Creek](/wiki/Wolli_Creek,_New_South_Wales "Wolli Creek, New South Wales"), and the construction of ventilation stacks required to remove pollution from the tunnel, which, ostensibly due to cost, lack filtration systems.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*] Since its opening, the M5 East has attracted criticism for its lack of capacity, being full from the day it was opened, given that the tunnels provide only two lanes of traffic in either direction, and no provision has been made for future expansion. Most congestion problems occur at the western end of the main tunnel, which is long and steep.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

An addition lane for slow traffic begins at the western tunnel exit for a short distance, to remove slow and heavy traffic. Had this been extended into the tunnel for a few hundred metres, the capacity would be far greater.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*] These steep entrances to the tunnelled section are also the site of frequent vehicle accidents, usually involving heavy traffic.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*] Pollution and smog inside the tunnel has also been a major concern, after it was revealed that the [New South Wales Department of Health](/wiki/New_South_Wales_Department_of_Health "New South Wales Department of Health") advised the [Roads & Traffic Authority](/wiki/Roads_%26_Traffic_Authority "Roads & Traffic Authority") that pollution levels in the tunnel exceeded acceptable limits during peak hours of use, and urged motorists using the tunnel to keep their windows up and putting their car's ventilation system on "recirculate".[[29]](#cite_note-31)

The M5 East has been beset by many closures, due to accidents and computer failures within the tunnel, which cause gridlock.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*] Between 2002 and 2008 there were six closures attributed to technical and computer failures alone in the tunnel.[[30]](#cite_note-SMH_Millions-32) The [Government of New South Wales](/wiki/Government_of_New_South_Wales "Government of New South Wales") must pay penalties to the operators of the M5 East, BHBB, due to more traffic using the M5 than originally forecast. The penalties were expected to amount to $13 million by 2011. Some of this increase in demand was caused by the State Government's "Cashback" scheme, which involves public money subsidising motorists for using the adjoining, tolled M5 South West Motorway.[[30]](#cite_note-SMH_Millions-32)

Both the South West Motorway (to its interchange with Metroad 9 along Narellan Road) and the M5 East were designated as Metroad 5. With the conversion to the newer alphanumeric system in 2013, Metroad 5 was truncated back to the [Roden Cutler Interchange](/wiki/Sir_Roden_Cutler_VC_Memorial_Interchange "Sir Roden Cutler VC Memorial Interchange") (the former allocation renamed Hume Motorway and allocated M31) and replaced with route M5.[[31]](#cite_note-33)

Between December 2011 and April 2020, the M5 East was operated and maintained by [Leighton](/wiki/CIMIC_Group "CIMIC Group") (and later [Ventia](/wiki/Ventia "Ventia")) on behalf of [Transport for NSW](/wiki/Transport_for_NSW "Transport for NSW").[[32]](#cite_note-34)[[28]](#cite_note-oldm5east-30) On 1 May 2020, [WestConnex](/wiki/WestConnex "WestConnex") took over the operations and maintenance of the tunnels.[[33]](#cite_note-35) Following the commencement of tolls in July 2020, the M5 East ceased being a freeway.

#### New M5

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=M5_Motorway_(Sydney)&action=edit&section=6 "Edit section: New M5")]

See also: [WestConnex](/wiki/WestConnex "WestConnex") and [M8 Motorway (Sydney)](/wiki/M8_Motorway_(Sydney) "M8 Motorway (Sydney)")

In 2009, the government released the M5 Transport Corridor Feasibility Study, which investigated strategic options for improving the M5 Motorway corridor. The study identified a preliminary preferred option, being the M5 East Duplication, consisted of duplicating the M5 East and construction of a new connection from the M5 East at Arncliffe to Euston Road, Qantas Drive and Gardeners Road.[[34]](#cite_note-36) The strategic concept for the M5 East Duplication was placed on public exhibition between November 2009 and March 2010 for community and stakeholder feedback.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*] Feedback received was used to further develop and refine the scheme. In 2012, the scheme become the King Georges Road Interchange Upgrade and New M5 projects, the second stage of WestConnex. The New M5 would consist of separate tunnels parallel to the M5 East tunnels and would later become known as [M8 Motorway](/wiki/M8_Motorway_(Sydney) "M8 Motorway (Sydney)").

The M8 tunnels were completed in July 2020. The tunnels connect to the existing M5 East at Kingsgrove and run roughly parallel to M5 East to [Rozelle Interchange](/wiki/Rozelle_Interchange "Rozelle Interchange"), forming an inner CBD bypass.

#### Toll

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=M5_Motorway_(Sydney)&action=edit&section=7 "Edit section: Toll")]

See also: [WestConnex § Tolls](/wiki/WestConnex#Tolls "WestConnex")

Since 5 July 2020, when the M8 tunnels opened, the majority of the M5 East between King Georges Road and Marsh Street, as well as the M8 Motorway, is tolled by distance travelled as part of the [WestConnex](/wiki/WestConnex "WestConnex").[[35]](#cite_note-37) A short section of the M5 East between Marsh Street and [General Holmes Drive](/wiki/General_Holmes_Drive "General Holmes Drive") remains toll-free.[[36]](#cite_note-wcx-38) As both the M5 East and M8 Motorway are tolled under WestConnex, a vehicle travelling on the M5 East (between King Georges Road and M8) and M8 together will only incur a single toll.[[37]](#cite_note-39)

The M5 East and M8 Motorway toll charge consists of:

* a [flagfall](/wiki/Flagfall "Flagfall")
* a charge per kilometre

Tolls for heavy vehicles are triple of cars and motorcycles. Toll prices increase by 4% or the consumer price index (CPI) every year, whichever is greater, until 2040, after which CPI will apply.[[36]](#cite_note-wcx-38)
